content: I presume this was an IMAP account. Moving from server to local storage, regardless of email client, can be problematic, since the network itself is involved. A transfer may contain message headers, but not the message content. Better to COPY to local folders so that you can verify content. I suggest restoring the recycle bin, verifying the message content is there, and then COPYing to local folders, and then rechecking. Personally, I have doubts on the trash folder, but am hoping for success. :)
creator: davidsk

content:
laurenmb5678 said
New user - I recently moved thousands of messages from my inbox to a local folder. At some point, Thunderbird asked me to compact folders and I said yes. I noticed today that I am now missing about 13 years worth of messages from my local inbox folder.

Did you move those messages from your Inbox folder to a new folder you created manually in your Local Folders? Whether you moved or copied messages from your Inbox to that local folder they should still be stored there. And even if you moved them from an IMAP Inbox to a local folder those messages should still be present in the local folder. Did you verify its contents?

I just tested your supposed moves in one of my Thunderbird profiles: • an IMAP account for which I disabled ⬜ "Keep messages in all folders for this account on this computer" in Account settings > account name > Synchronisation & Storage > Message Synchronising ...(see first image) • then I created a new folder "Komoot" in my Local Folders • in my IMAP Inbox I selected all messages I'd received from Komoot, right-clicked on the selection and chose "Move to > Local Folders > Komoot • all selected messages were moved to folder Komoot in Local Folders (see second image) and had subsequently disappeared from Inbox

Let's suppose you moved messages from an Inbox in a POP account to Local Folders. In that case the messages would be stored in the folder in Local Folders and disappear from the Inbox. But the moved messages would still be stored in the Inbox file in your Thunderbird profile folder unless you compact the Inbox folder >>>> https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/compacting-folders So I still don't understand why your moved messages would no longer be stored in that local folder in your Local Folders ...

content:
Please quote any and all error messages you see, completely and verbatim. Screen shots would be OK.

The fact that NONE of your devices can send or receive suggests that it's not specifically a Thunderbird (or Apple or Linux) problem, but an internet connection problem (which points the finger at Roadrunner/Spectrum.)

The first thing to do would be to confirm that you're using the right server settings. I found this page that covers the topic in excruciating detail, I guess because of the legacy of all their takeovers and name changes.

It could be a temporary problem on their end that could go away on its own given some time (cold comfort, I know.) It could help to reboot your gateway/router device and all your computers, too. A router update, pushed by Spectrum, could potentially disrupt service.
creator: rednoise

content:
I have no personal experience with Proton Mail, but I do know that Thunderbird cannot interact directly with it. It (and other IMAP/SMTP email clients) require "Proton Mail Bridge", a program that acts as an intermediary between Thunderbird and the Internet. The basic idea seems to be that you set up Thunderbird to address Proton Mail Bridge, which in turn talks to the Proton Mail servers.

Here's an article about it:.

https://proton.me/support/imap-smtp-and-pop3-setup

Some other info pages about the Mail Bridge:

https://proton.me/support/mail
creator: rednoise

content:
If all accounts are IMAP, all remote folders will replicate with all local installations. Not Local Folders, though - they are all independent and reside only on the individual computer. Here's an article that describes in good detail what IMAP is and how it works:

https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/imap-synchronization
